--- SOURCE 1: Comparing two forms of government: The Unitary and The ... ---
URL: https://www.lawjournal.info/article/169/5-1-19-460.pdf

SUMMARY:
Unitary systems feature centralization of power, while federal systems emphasize a division of powers.

FULL CONTENT:
~ 115 ~ International Journal of Law, Justice and Jurisprudence 2025; 5(1): 115-118 E-ISSN: 2790-0681 P-ISSN: 2790-0673 www.lawjournal.info IJLJJ 2025; 5(1): 115-118 Received: 17-12-2024 Accepted: 22-01-2025 Dr. SK Bose Professor, School of Law, Manav Rachna University, Faridabad, Haryana, India Dr. Aditi Chaudhary School of Law, Manav Rachna University, Faridabad, Haryana, India Vaibhav Rajan Tyagi LLM, School of Law, Manav Rachna University, Faridabad, Haryana, India Correspondence Author: Dr. SK Bose Professor, School of Law, Manav Rachna University, Faridabad, Haryana, India Comparing two forms of government: The Unitary and The Federal SK Bose, Dr. Aditi Chaudhary and Vaibhav Rajan Tyagi DOI: https://www.doi.org/10.22271/2790-0673.2025.v5.i1b.169 Abstract Governance structures globally can be broadly categorized into unitary and federal systems, each with distinct characteristics and implications for political organization and citizen engagement. The dichotomy of political systems between unitary and federal form exist in most of the states in the present era of political globalization. In a unitary government all the powers are vested in the central government whereas in a federal form of government the powers are bifurcated between the center and the state. Both governance models have their advantages and disadvantages, affecting how policies are implemented and how citizens engage with their government. A unitary system centralizes authority within a single national government. In this framework, local governments may exist, but they primarily serve as extensions of the central authority, executing decisions made by the national parliament. In contrast, a federal system disperses power across multiple layers of government, typically involving a national and several regional or state governments. This arrangement allows for greater local autonomy and responsiveness to regional needs, fostering a sense of participation and representation among diverse populations. This research article highlights a comparative analysis between a unitary and federal structure of government in a state. It focuses to examine the dichotomy of unitary and federal forms of governance which exist in the present. It also shows an availability of abundance of laws for the welfare of human beings in both the countries. The research suggests application of unitary or federal polity through comparative assessment of the concept, features, and governance of both the system in a state. Keywords: Unitary government, federal government, states governance, form of government Introduction The concepts of unitary and federal forms of government are fundamental to the organization and distribution of political power within a state. Both systems have their own structures, advantages, and disadvantages, affecting how laws and policies are created and implemented. In a unitary system, the central government holds the majority of the power. This means that regional or local governments derive their authority from the central government and can be created or dissolved at its discretion. Laws and policies are generally uniform across the country, allowing for consistent governance. Countries like U.S.A, France, Japan, and the United Kingdom operate under a unitary system, where the central government exercises significant control over local jurisdictions. Whereas, in a federal system, power is constitutionally divided between the central government and regional governments (states or provinces). Each level of government has its own responsibilities and authority. States or provinces have the power to legislate and govern in certain areas independently of the central government, leading to diversity in laws and governance. Unitary systems feature centralization of power, while federal systems emphasize a division of powers. In a unitary government, local authorities can have their powers revoked, whereas in a federal system, the constitution typically protects the powers of both central and state/provincial governments. The choice between a unitary and federal system of government often depends on a country's geographic, cultural, and historical context. While unitary governments may provide streamlined governance, federal systems allow for greater representation of diverse populations. Understanding these forms of government is essential for analyzing how different countries approach governance and the allocation of power. The research paper will highlights the comparative analysis of unitary and federal form of government adopted, established, and applied in the governance by most of the states in the global world.
International Journal of Law, Justice and Jurisprudence https://www.lawjournal.info ~ 116 ~ The paper will show the assessment of governance by the two different system of polity i.e. unitary and federal system of governance. The comparison shall focus on the understanding of how different countries govern and manage their political, legal, and administrative affairs. Statement of problem The research is conducted to analyze the concept of unitary and federal form of government in the global world. Research problem is to find out which form of government performs better in regulation and governance of the country. Review of literature Elazar, D. J. (1997). Contrasting Unitary and Federal Systems. International Political Science Review / Revue Internationale de Science Politique, 18(3), 237–251. http://www.jstor.org/stable/1601342. Accessed on 20 Sept.2024 The research article highlights that how in the modern age most of the states have divided themselves and have shaped the polities into unitary and federal forms. The author speaks the states in dividing themselves into unitary and federal polities have obscure the three major models of polity which speak about hierarchic, organic, and covenantal which forms the origin and development of polity. The article examines the cultural, behavioural, and institutional outcomes from the models of polity and compare them with one another. Barrios-Suvelza, F. X. (2020). Neither unitary nor federal: Did Bolivians invent something new? International Political Science Review / Revue Internationale de Science Politique, 41(3), 402–418. https://www.jstor.org/stable/26956850. Accessed on 20 Sept.2024 The author speaks about the systematic classification of the form of states by the standard means of unitary and federal form. The research article highlights that the constitutional changes in Bolivia have challenged the long-standing assumptions that a unitary-federal divide best serves the goal of an accurate categorization of form of states in the field of comparative federalism. The research article shed light on the constitutional reforms adopted by Bolivia which has resulted in the settled framework of comparative federalism to squeezing Bolivia into unitary form. The research article attempts to test out a new classificatory framework for state forms with the use legal theory and methodological tools of political science. Research Questions  What is a unitary form of government?  What is a federal form of government?  What is the comparative analysis of a government based on unitary and federal forms?  What are the remedies and suggestions for the application of unitary and federal form of government? Research objectives The research has been done to achieve the following objectives: 1. To research about the concept of unitary form of government. 2. To research about the concept of federal form of government. 3. To highlight a comparative study of government based on unitary and federal forms. 4. To provide the suggestions and remedies regarding for the application of unitary and federal form of government. Research Methodology In this research doctrinal research method is followed throughout the research article. An analysis is conducted on primary and secondary data. Primary data includes statutes, case laws while secondary data includes articles, blogs, websites, and journals. All these data has been used to develop an understanding relating to the title of research article. Hypotheses The federal form of government is better than unitary form of government. Does the two forms of government can share certain aspects or characteristics of each other. Scope of the study This study extends to the comparative analysis of unitary and federal form of government in states. It focuses on the comparatively assessing the dichotomy of the unitary and federal form of government and highlights which form of governance is better for the country. Limitation of the study The study is limited in aspects of accurate data collection in the research. The collection of data is difficult in analyzing all the human rights laws between both the countries and in highlighting the difference of laws between both the countries. Unitary form of government A political system where all the power of the state are vested and exercised through a central authority is one which is known as the unitary system of governance in a state. To understand the characteristics of a unitary government the major contribution comes from the elements that a unitary government is a simple system where single government at the center is vested with centralized administrative powers over the whole nation. In this type of political system, the central government has a constitutional valid status as the constitution itself provide the central government with all the powers of the legislation, executive, judiciary in a nation. In the system of unitary governance, the powers are not shared by the other subunits at the regional or local level of governance. Another key element of a unitary system is the unification of laws as the laws are made by the single government at the central level for the whole state. The laws are applicable to every territory of the state uniformly unlike in a federation. The unitary system does not bifurcate its powers between the center and the other provinces of the state. Here the sole governing body is the central government. Flexibility of the constitution in a unitary set up is another key feature of this political system. The dynamic nature of the constitution makes it easy to amend and change as per the needs of the thread of time and circumstances. Through the passage of time there may come many changes in the society therefore the flexible nature of the constitution aid in the evolution of power, rights, limitations etc. provided by the constitution to the citizens as well as other persons residing in the nation in a unitary political system. International Journal of Law, Justice and Jurisprudence https://www.lawjournal.info ~ 117 ~ Unitary system of government is one of the principal forms of state governance, along with federal systems where power is constitutionally divided between national and subnational governments. Each system has its own set of advantages and challenges that shape the political and administrative landscape of the country. Federal form of government Federal system of government in a political system signifies that system in which the powers are bifurcated between the central and the state government in a nation. Federal structure shows a system in which powers are divided between the central, regional, and local government. The hierarchy of government in a nation where federal structure exists provides for the central government at the national level, state government at the state or regional level, and the local government at the local level. Central government execute powers and responsibilities at the national level keeping in mind the development and security of the whole nation. Whereas the state government at the provincial or regional level is accountable for the administration of the society in the specified region or state. The local government is responsible for the governance at the local level of territory which include district, town, block, village. Federal political system is a combination of different government which share powers among them over the administration of the society in the same territory of the whole nation. Both the central as well as the state or regional government are empowered and recognized by the constitution of the nation in the federal set up. The federal structure in the present era share many diverse and different form of government in many nations which have adopted the federal political system in the global world. The concept of federalism is prominent in the federal form of government in a state. The concept speak about the mode of government which include within it the central government as well the state or regional government (subunits, territorial, provincial government) in a single political system of governance which govern through bifurcation of powers between the two governments. Some of the nations in the global world where the concept of federalism is prominent include Russia, United States of America (U.S.A), Australia, Argentina, Belgium, Canada, etc. India is also a part of the federal political system but it also share unitary features because of which it is called a quasi-federal political structure. Comparative analysis of government based on unitary and federal system The constitution of every nation provide for certain political system which lay out the design for the smooth functioning of the government in that nation. Unitary, federal, and the combination of both unitary and federal features are the political system which are adopted by the nations through their constitution. In order to clearly understand the concept of different political system which are applied in the different forms of government a comparative table is given below to give a clear distinction between the unitary and federal political system. Table 1: Comparison Between Unitary and Federal Political Systems Unitary system Federal system The sovereign authority is the central government. The sovereign authority divide its power between the central and state government. Powers are vested in the central government in this type of political system. Powers are shared between the Central and state government in this type of political system. In a unitary system a single Government exist in a nation. In federal system two government i.e. Dual government at different level co-exist in one nation. In the unitary system laws are made in uniformity which are applicable in the whole nation. In federal system laws or statutes are made and passed by both the central government as well as the state government which are applicable in the specified territories. In countries which are small in respect of their territorial area are best suited for the unitary system of governance. Countries which are big in respect of their territorial area are best suited for the federal system of governance in the nation. In the unitary system the constitution is the supreme authority. In the federal system the supremacy of constitution is not guaranteed. A few examples of countries which have a unitary system are China, France, Japan, United Kingdom (U.K.) Etc. Countries which follow the federal system of governance are Pakistan, Brazil, Russia, Etc. The above table shows the comparative analysis between the unitary and federal system of governance. It shows the dichotomy between the two political systems of governance. It highlights the key features and some countries which follow either of the two system i.e. unitary or federal in their respective areas. Remedies and Suggestions Remedies and Suggestions for Unitary Governments 1. Decentralization of power: Implement frameworks for devolution of powers to local governments to empower them in decision-making processes and address local issues effectively. 2. Public Participation: Develop public consultation and participation in policymaking at the local level to ensure that the needs and preferences of citizens are considered. 3. Balanced Resource Allocation: Ensure fair and balanced allocation of resources to different regions to prevent disparities and support economic development in less developed areas. 4. Strengthening Local Governance: Enhance the capacity and competencies of local governments through training, resources, and legal frameworks to enable them to take on more responsibilities. 5. Regular Feedback Mechanisms: Create platforms for continuous feedback from local communities to the central government to address issues and adapt policies accordingly. 6. Emergency Powers Limitations: Clearly define the extent and limits of emergency powers to prevent potential misuse by the central government. International Journal of Law, Justice and Jurisprudence https://www.lawjournal.info ~ 118 ~ Remedies and Suggestions for Federal Governments 1. Clear Constitutional Framework: Establish clear delineations of powers and responsibilities between the federal and state/provincial governments to reduce conflicts and overlap. 2. Strengthen Intergovernmental Relations: Foster cooperative mechanisms for interaction between different levels of government to address shared concerns and promote collaboration. 3. Conflict Resolution Mechanisms: Create formal processes for resolving disputes between national and regional governments, possibly through judicial interpretation or negotiation frameworks. 4. Equitable Resource Distribution: Develop fair systems for resource allocation and taxation to ensure equitable development across regions to prevent disparities. 5. Local Autonomy: Promote awareness and understanding of federalism among citizens to encourage active participation in the democratic process and appreciation of local governance. 6. Engage in Civic Education: Ensure law enforcement agencies are sensitive to the needs and rights of local communities and adapt policies to local contexts rather than imposing uniform federal standards. 7. Responsive Policing and Law Enforcement: Ensure law enforcement agencies are sensitive to the needs and rights of local communities and adapt policies to local contexts rather than imposing uniform federal standards. 8. Regular Reviews and Reforms: Periodically review the federal structure and policies to adapt to changing social, economic, and political dynamics. By adopting these remedies and suggestions, both unitary and federal governments can improve their effectiveness, responsiveness, and relevance to the people they serve. Conclusion In conclusion, the choice between a unitary and a federal form of government significantly influences the structure, function, and effectiveness of governance within a state. Each system has its unique strengths and weaknesses that cater to different political, cultural, and historical landscapes. Unitary systems centralize authority and decision-making powers within a central government, which can lead to greater uniformity in laws and policies across the entire country, whereas the Federal systems distribute power between a central authority and various regional governments, allowing for greater local governance and representation of diverse populations. Ultimately, the effectiveness of either system depends on the particular context of a country, including its size, diversity, historical context, and the specific needs of its population. Some nations may benefit from the cohesiveness of a unitary system, while others may find that the flexibility of federalism better accommodates their diverse interests. There is no argument which gives the answer to decide the best system of governance in a nation as it greatly depends on various factors of that nation. A unitary system maybe more effective in a smaller country or in a state which wants a strong central governance, whereas a federal system is beneficial in larger more diverse nations where regional and local governance is also required to achieve the goal of political governance. Ultimately, the selection of a governmental system should reflect the values, needs, and aspirations of the people it serves. Both the forms of government can share and include the features or characteristics of the opposite system as seen in the governance of India which is stated to be a quasi-federal form of government as it shares some feature of unitary form of government and also runs on the doctrine of separation of powers. Thus, India is a quasi-federal state which have adopted the federal system of governance which also shares some of the features of unitary form of government. The research paper shows the comparative analysis of the unitary as well as federal system of governance. The choice between a unitary and federal system of government depends largely on historical, cultural, and social contexts. While unitary systems may foster unity and efficiency, federal systems can enhance local autonomy and democratic representation. Each approach has its unique merits and challenges, suggesting that the effectiveness of governance is ultimately shaped by how well the chosen system aligns with the needs and values of its society. Thus, nations must carefully evaluate their specific circumstances to determine the most suitable governmental structure. The research outlines the distinctive characteristics of both the system of government in a state. It focuses on these characteristics which convey the better governance of a system in different circumstances. References 1. Elazar DJ. Contrasting Unitary and Federal Systems. --- SOURCE 3: Constitutional law - Unitary, Federal, Systems ---
URL: https://www.britannica.com/topic/constitutional-law/Unitary-and-federal-systems

SUMMARY:
Depending on how a constitution organizes power between the central and subnational governments, a country may be said to possess either a unitary or a federal system (see also federalism). The model federal state is characterized by the existence, at the national level, of a written, rigid constitution guaranteeing the several intermediate governments not only permanence and independence but also a full complement of legislative, executive, and judicial powers. The national constitution enumerates the powers granted to the central government; the remaining powers are reserved to the intermediate governments at the state or provincial level. States or provinces in federal systems also have their own constitutions that define the institutions of their respective governments, as well as the powers that are devolved further to their local governments.

# Unitary and federal systems

Our editors will review what you’ve submitted and determine whether to revise the article.

## The distinction between unitary and federal [states](https://www.britannica.com/topic/state-sovereign-political-entity)

No modern [country](https://www.britannica.com/topic/nation-state) can be [governed](https://www.britannica.com/topic/government) from a single location only. The affairs of municipalities and rural areas must be left to the administration of [local governments](https://www.britannica.com/topic/local-government). Accordingly, all countries have at least two levels of government: central and local. A number of countries also contain a third level of government, which is responsible for the interests of more or less large regions.

The distribution of powers between different levels of government is an important aspect of the [constitutional](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/constitutional) organization of a state. Among states with two levels of government, distinctions can be made on the basis of the greater or lesser [autonomy](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/autonomy) granted to the local level. The British government’s respect for local self-government has always been a characteristic of its [constitution](https://www.britannica.com/topic/constitution-politics-and-law). In contrast, [France](https://www.britannica.com/place/France) traditionally had kept its local authorities under strict central control. In countries with three levels of government, the distribution of powers between the central and the intermediate governments varies. States formed through the union of formerly independent states usually maintain an intermediate level with considerable legislative, [executive](https://www.britannica.com/topic/executive-government), and judicial powers (as in the [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States), Argentina, and Switzerland), though some grant few powers to this level. The latter situation occurs often in countries that have introduced the intermediate level as a correction to their previous choice of two levels—as Italy did in its constitution of 1948 and Spain in its constitution of 1978.

Depending on how a constitution organizes power between the central and subnational governments, a country may be said to possess either a [unitary](https://www.britannica.com/topic/unitary-state) or a [federal](https://www.britannica.com/topic/federalism) system (*see also* [federalism](https://www.britannica.com/topic/federalism)). In a unitary system the only level of government besides the central is the local or [municipal government](https://www.britannica.com/topic/local-government). Although local governments may enjoy considerable autonomy, their powers are not accorded constitutional status; the central government determines which decisions to “devolve” to the local level and may abolish local governments if it so chooses. In federal systems there is an intermediate level of governmental authority between the central and the local; it usually consists of states or provinces, though other entities (e.g., cantons or republics) may exist in some countries. Aside from the number of levels, the most important distinction between a unitary system and a federal one is that the states or provinces of a federal state have constitutionally protected [sovereignty](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/sovereignty). Within a federal system the state or provincial governments share sovereignty with the central government and have final [jurisdiction](https://www.britannica.com/topic/jurisdiction) over a broad range of policy areas.

Federal and unitary systems are ideal types, representing the endpoints of a [continuum](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/continuum). Most countries fall somewhere in between the two extremes—states can be more or less unitary or more or less federal. So-called “semifederal” countries occupy a middle category, possessing an intermediate level of government that does not have the same protections of sovereignty that the states or provinces of federal states enjoy.

A proper understanding of these types of constitution requires the consideration of additional features of each type. The model [federal state](https://www.britannica.com/topic/federal-state) is characterized by the existence, at the national level, of a written, rigid constitution guaranteeing the several intermediate governments not only permanence and independence but also a full complement of legislative, executive, and judicial powers. The national constitution enumerates the powers granted to the central government; the remaining powers are reserved to the intermediate governments at the state or provincial level. These subnational entities are generally represented at the national level, possibly on an equal footing, in a second chamber of the national [legislature](https://www.britannica.com/topic/legislature) (often called the upper house, or senate). They also often are central to the process of [amending](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/amending) the national constitution. For example, some number of state or provincial legislatures may be required to consent to the ratification of [amendments](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/amendments) passed by the federal legislature. States or provinces in federal systems also have their own constitutions that define the institutions of their respective governments, as well as the powers that are devolved further to their local governments. Such constitutional arrangements are a guarantee against possible efforts of the central government to enlarge its jurisdiction and so imperil the important political role that intermediate governments play in a federal system. More than formal constitutional safeguards are required to preserve that role. Apart from constitutional amendments, the central government may seek to broaden its own powers through the use of constitutional clauses granting “implied powers.” In some federal states (e.g., Argentina and India), there are emergency provisions by which the central government may suspend the powers of individual state or provincial governments. If abused, these provisions—meant to be used only in cases of rebellion or other severe disturbance against the constitutional order—may seriously compromise the constitutionally enshrined principle of shared sovereignty that is the hallmark of federalism. Even in established federal [democracies](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/democracies) (e.g., Canada, Germany, and the United States), the exact distribution of powers between levels of government is a matter of constant dispute between central and subnational governments. Disputes about federal-state matters are often the subject of rulings in courts or constitutional tribunals or conferences involving the heads of the central and subnational governments.

Semifederal states are also based, as a rule, on rigid written constitutions granting some limited legislative and administrative (though seldom judicial) powers to the intermediate or regional governments. But because regional governments in semifederal states possess [jurisdiction](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/jurisdiction) only over enumerated matters (and even here they are subject in part to the overriding powers of the central authorities), their actual role and political influence within the system largely depend on the tendency of the central government to buttress or to restrict their autonomy. Where the powers granted by the constitution to the regional governments are particularly minimal, the semifederal state will look in many respects like a unitary state. Where the powers are relatively large and the central government favours their expansion—perhaps because the central government is itself a coalition of national and regional parties—the state tends to assume federal characteristics, even if the typical hallmarks of the federal system are not present. Spain and Belgium are good examples of semifederal states that have become increasingly more federal in practice.

--- SOURCE 4: UNITARY AND FEDERAL GOVERNMENT ---
URL: https://www.shivajicollege.ac.in/sPanel/uploads/econtent/100863cb824066de18226c356c984bc0.pdf

SUMMARY:
In a unitary government, all the powers of government are vested in the central government whereas in a federal government, the powers of government are divided

FULL CONTENT:
Page 1 of 4 UNITARY AND FEDERAL GOVERNMENT On the basis of relationship between the centre and the units, the governments may be classified as unitary and federal. In a unitary government, all the powers of government are vested in the central government whereas in a federal government, the powers of government are divided between the centre and the units. Their distinctive feature and comparative merits and demerits are given as follows. UNITARY GOVERNMENT Unitary government is a kind of government system in which a single power, which is known as the central government, controls the whole government. In fact, all powers and administrative divisions authorities lies at the central place. Today most of the government systems in the world are based on unitary system of government. It is slightly different from federal model of government. In unitary government, central government has the power to increase or curtail the power of subnational units. It can create and abolished the same. UK, Afghanistan, Italy, China, Saudi Arabia, Spain, etc., are the important examples of unitary government. The unitary government system is based on the concept of consistency, unity, and identity that’s why the centralization of power and authority system remains at the top priority. The decision-making power rests with the central government that are shared by the government with the lower level government when needed. There are not so many options for change and new innovation as the people have a very limited voice in this government system. There are many merits and demerits of unitary government. It is useful in the term that rules and regulations in this government systems remain consist and equal throughout the country. Moreover, it is less expensive as compared to the federal government because the number of powerful people remains very low. In a time of emergency, it makes timely decisions as compared to the federal government system. But at the same time, the concept of freedom of speech and expression always remains at a low priority that’s why most of the principles of unitary government are much similar to that of dictatorship system of government. Its distinctive feature is given as follows; 1. Concentration of Powers: A unitary government is one in which all the powers of administration are vested in a single centre. The centre is omnipotent. A unitary state may be divided into small units for the sake of administrative convenience but the units do not have any constitutional status of their own. Page 2 of 4 In other words, the constitution does not confer any powers on the units. It is the central government which dele gates certain powers to the units on its own accord. The units are, therefore, subordinate agents of centre. The powers enjoyed by them are the gifts of the centre and as such these can be taken back at any moment. The units are thus not autonomous and independent in any way. 2. Single Government: In a unitary government, there is a single set of governmental apparatus. There is a single supreme legislature, single executive body and one supreme judiciary. England, for example, is a unitary state. She has one parliament as her legislature, the King-in-Council as the executive and the judicial committee of the House of Lords as her supreme judiciary. 3. Written or unwritten Constitution: A unitary government may or may not have a writ ten constitution. As for example, England and France are unitary states. France has a written constitution but England has none 4. Rigid or Flexible Constitution: Unlike a federation, a unitary state may or may not have a rigid constitution, e.g., the constitution of England is flexible but that of France is slightly rigid. 5. No Special Judiciary: There is no need of having a special judiciary with wide powers of judicial veto in a unitary government. Even the highest court of U.K., for example, cannot sit in judgment over the law passed by Parliament. FEDERAL GOVERNMENT Federal government is a type of national government in which government have powers to delegates the power to other elected member of the states. There can be two level of federal government in a country either it is performing through common institutions or through powers as prescribed by a constitution of the state. It is totally opposite to the unitary government. In federation or federal government, provinces or territories enjoys some rights as are available to the independent states. However international diplomacy, national security, Page 3 of 4 foreign affairs and other kinds of international dealings are solely made by the federal government. Pakistan, India, Brazil, Switzerland, Australia, Belgium, Canada, etc., are the significant examples of federal government. Mostly the federal government system is referred to the United States government. This government is based on the republicanism and federalism. In the federal system, power is jointly shared between the state and federal governments. In the federal government system, the powers never rest with one national government. However, there can be certain powers and authorities that remain totally with the federal government like policies on defence, budget, international diplomacy, etc. The hierarchy of power in federal government system starts from the federal level and then flows to the state and then local level. Its distinctive feature is given as follows; 1. Division of Powers: In a federal government the powers of administration are divided between the centre and the units. The powers may be distributed in two different ways. Either the constitution states what powers the federal authority shall have, and leaves the remainder to the federating units, or it states what powers the federating units shall possess and leaves the remainder to the federal authority. The remainder is generally known as residuary powers. The first method was employed in America and the second in Canada. The federal government in U.S.A., for example, is weak in relation to the states whereas the federal government in Canada is more powerful. In a federation both the federal and state governments are independent and autonomous in the spheres of their powers. 'One is not subordinate to the other. Both derive their powers from the constitution which is the supreme law of the land. The powers enjoyed by the units are, therefore, original and not delegated by the centre. 2. Separate Government: In a federal form of government both the centre and the units have their separate set of governmental apparatus. America is a federation of states. States have therefore separate legislatures and Separate executives. 3. Written Constitution: A federal government must have a written constitution. As a federation is a political partnership of various states and consequently there must be a written agreement in the form of a written constitution. Page 4 of 4 4. Rigid Constitution: The constitution of a federation should be more or less rigid. It is regarded as a sacred agreement, the spirit of which should not be easily violated. A flexible constitution allows a scope to the central government to curtail the autonomy of the federating states. 5. Special Judiciary: In a federation, there are possibilities of constitutional disputes arising between the federal centre and the units or between one unit and another. All these disputes are to be adjudicated in the light of the constitution. For this purpose a special judiciary with wide powers must be established. It should act as the custodian and guardian of the constitution. It should be vested with powers of declaring any law, national or local, ultra vires if it is at variance with the articles of the constitution. The constitution is thus the supreme law in a federation to which both the centric and the state must adhere to.

Please use the tabs to search for**Books**, **Articles**, **Web Resources**, and **Videos**. If you have any questions, do not hesitate to contact the Skyline College Librarians.

The three Systems
-----------------

**[Federal System](https://www.britannica.com/topic/federalism)**

Power is shared by a powerful central government and states or provinces that are given considerable self-rule, usually through their own legislatures.

 Examples: The United States, Australia, the Federal Republic of Germany.

![Image 2: Federal System](http://lgimages.s3.amazonaws.com/data/imagemanager/70324/federal.gif)**[Unitary System](https://www.britannica.com/topic/unitary-system)**

One central government controls weaker states. Power is not shared between states, counties, or provinces.

 Examples: China, United Kingdom (although Scotland has been granted self-rule).

![Image 3: Unitary System](http://lgimages.s3.amazonaws.com/data/imagemanager/70324/unitary.gif)**[Confederal System](https://www.britannica.com/topic/confederation-politics)** Weak or loose organization of states agrees to follow a powerful central government. Nations can choose to follow or not follow the lead of the weak central government. Examples: The Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS), formerly known as the Soviet Union. Also, Switzerland's canton system and the Confederate States of America (1861-1865). 
![Image 4: Confederation System](http://lgimages.s3.amazonaws.com/data/imagemanager/70324/confederation.gif)

Click on the links here to read articles about the difference between[confederation and federation](https://www.britannica.com/topic/political-system/Confederations-and-federations#ref416900)and the difference between[unitary and federal states](https://www.britannica.com/topic/constitutional-law/Unitary-and-federal-systems#toc22075)from Encyclopedia Britannica.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Unitary systems centralize political power within a single national authority, exemplified by countries like France and Japan, ensuring uniform laws and policies across all regions.
Federal systems, such as those in the United States and Germany, divide powers between national and regional governments, allowing local authorities to legislate and govern independently.

In confederal systems, like the former Confederate States of America, sovereignty resides primarily with regional entities, often resulting in weak central governance and challenges in policy implementation.
Unitary systems may limit citizen participation due to centralized decision-making, whereas federal systems encourage greater local involvement, enhancing democratic engagement and representation.
The effectiveness of governance varies; unitary systems may provide swift decision-making, while federal systems offer diverse representation, each suited to different societal needs and contexts.
